articles.title: The Russells Did Not Go to Church
articles.author: Edith Russell
articles.text:
The Russells Did Not Go to Church
Chapter 2
Edith Russell

Synopsis: The Russells, who live on the edge of the Haworth moors in England, do not go to church. However, the daughter Edith almost unconsciously seeks a religious faith to satisfy her spiritual longing. She decides to attend a meeting in the nearby church.

THE vicarage was a large, grim house setting its back against the wide, bleak vista of the Haworth moors. The door boasted no bell, but a great iron knocker. It was wet and chilly in my hand and I let it fall with a harsh metallic thud which seemed to place an intolerable weight upon my spirits.

It seemed ages had elapsed before the door was opened and the thin, old face of a woman appeared to inquire my business.

"The Reverend Pratt asked me to come to a meeting of the new churchworkers' guild, tonight," I told her.

Hurriedly she opened the door wide enough to admit me. "Cum in! Cum in! afore all of us freeze to death!"

I stepped inside. I heard the door close dully behind me and the old woman led the way down a dark, stone-flagged hall, to a room at the furthest end, the door of which she thrust open to announce:

"A young leddy fer't meating."

I saw a group of perhaps a dozen individuals all sitting within the feeble glow of an oil lamp, all manifestly chilled by the gusty windswept night and the patent inadequacy of a small coal fire smoldering in the grate. I recognized two of the men as neighboring farmers and a large woman with a walking cane as a "ruling hand" in the village. No one spoke to me as I entered.

Everyone was apparently awaiting the arrival of the vicar and the only sensation I seemed to evoke was one of general disappointment. The reverend gentleman was late. One of the farmers took out his watch—a great silver timepiece with a sonorous tick—and consulted it grimly.

"Five an' twenty t'eight an' 't parson noan 'ere yit! Ahm noan barn ta wait much longer."

This he uttered in tones implying a serious threat. No one else spoke. It needed but five minutes to eight o'clock when the outside door clanged again and a moment later, an icy draught blew a somewhat disheveled parson into the room. His perfectly white hair was wet and windblown as though he had walked far without a hat. His greetings were unexpectedly warm, effusive, almost, and after the previous frigidity manifest in the room, a trifle embarrassing. He walked briskly about the room, rubbing his hands, belatedly seeing to everyone's comfort, remembering everyone's name and calling each by it. He was glad I had kept my promise to come. I thanked him for his courtesy. I was waiting to see what in him the villagers had stoned.

HE opened the meeting with prayer. It was a fine, eloquent speech and it made me recollect that the man who offered it was something of a scholar; that he quoted Sassoon frequently from the pulpit and that he was writing an autobiography of the Brontes. I thought he was not unlike the Reverend Patrick Bronte, himself—the same unrelenting eyes and thin mouth.

He finished his address to the Almighty with a request that Hannah Shuttleworth of Riddings Farm should be brought, by the whisperings of the Spirit, to realize her unworthiness to belong to St. Matthews since the birth of her illegitimate child, and thus save him, Parson Pratt, the necessity for dismissing her from the congregation.

I opened my eyes, prematurely, at this and found that everyone else, with the exception of Parson Pratt, had ceased trying to keep his closed long ago. They were looking at the devout gentleman, still in prayerful communion, with undisguised admiration. The parson's command of words impressed them visibly.

The object of our meeting had been the organization of a churchworkers' guild. The church needed workers, and so did Mr. Pratt, but, he had a great respect for organization. There must be a leader and someone who could write letters and make tea. It was gently hinted, too, that there must also be a few members in such an organization who had "long stockings" or a mattress or some other personal hiding place where the rich and eccentric keep their inheritances. The whole thing must be efficiently run in the approved manner if it were to be a success.

There must, of course, be a unity of feeling and purpose among the members. Everyone must lend his whole heart and soul to this first great project of the guild, which was the cleaning out—a moral purging—of St. Matthews.

"I'm sure you will all agree with me that it is in the best interests of the church, that such people should be disfellowshipped with all possible speed," he answered referring to the afore-mentioned Hannah.

"Why don't yer turn 'er out, Vicar?" a woman's voice demanded. "Why doan't yer insult 'er an' let 'er knaw she's noan wanted?"

"I might yet, Mrs. Drake. I might yet," Mr. Pratt said.

A cold wind blew from the hall under the door at my feet and outside the great door swung a moment then clanged shut, causing the whole house to shudder. With ridiculous optimism it flashed across my mind that God had come; that he wouldn't stand for it a moment longer and had come to justify himself. I waited, one minute, two minutes—but nothing happened; nothing except a voice, curiously like my own, voicing an aching, throbbing protest I thought I never could utter.

"Mr. Pratt!"

He was arrested in his perambulations. He stood looking down upon me, curious.

"Mr. Pratt," I plunged, desperately, "I don't know anything about Christianity—lots of people don't—but, but, supposing some poor unhappy soul were looking for Jesus and had been standing outside this room tonight, listening to all this, do you think it would have been any inducement for him to come in?"

I knew it was appallingly weak, but I had an aching lump in my breast which pumped determination, a queer feeling of love, and the instinct to fight and give battle into every vein in my body. A dozen pairs of eyes focused themselves upon me.

"I fear you have misunderstood me, Miss Russell," he replied, icily. "It is obvious that you are not aware of the character of some of these villagers. Nothing is sacred to them—neither the ornaments of the altar, nor my person. Are you aware that one of them actually stole a candlestick from off the Easter altar?"

I felt a fleeting surprise that this man had evidently never appreciated Hugo's Les Miserables. Otherwise he would not have afforded me such an excellent opportunity to quote the classic example of the Bishop and Jean Val Jean.

"The Bishop forgave Val Jean; made him a present of the candlesticks which he had stolen and brought another soul to God. You see," I told the furious vicar, "Val Jean could resist anything—cruelty, injustice, the beastliness of the galleys—anything at all, except love.

"The villagers are rough and many of them are ignorant," I said, "but they are not bad. They are like children who do not understand—who need to be shown. You do not love them, Parson Pratt. You have never even tried to understand them. You despise their ignorance, you don't pity it or try to change it. Hannah Shuttleworth is not a wicked woman—she simply doesn't know any better and doesn't care to know, because the people who have tried to teach her despised her first—despised her and condemned her. Christ forgave the woman who had sinned, he did not condemn her."

I was awfully near to tears and I swallowed desperately and blew my nose.

"Miss Russell!" The vicar's voice was bland again. "I am afraid you are too sentimental. You make Christianity sound like a love story...."

"It is a love story, Mr. Pratt," I broke in, urgently, "The greatest ever told."

THE Reverend Pratt ignored the interruption and surveyed me and the group of startled parishioners with a look depicting an attitude of magnificent fairness.

"I can see you mean well," he conceded, generously. "You wish to serve God. But—er, not here. You should give your talents to a city poor law mission. You would, I am sure, be very welcome there."

The inference was obvious. A sense of defeat swept over me as I remembered the country folk of the village, their clean, old-fashioned homes, their uncultivated voices, their superstitions. They believed in God, but they didn't understand Sassoon.

Looking into the faces of the now silent group, I waited for some feeling of bitterness to well up in my heart. But it didn't come. Instead, all I was aware of was the unutterable pathos and tragedy of it all. I wondered what the world would be like if all the clever people could just learn to be kind.

And it was suddenly borne upon me that God was here, after all. He must have slipped in once when the door clanged. I could not doubt it though I tried. His voice was in my ears; his love was swelling in my heart; the vision of him was in my eyes.

"You understand, Miss Russell," Mr. Pratt was continuing, "we cannot ask you now to be a member of the guild after tonight. We must have unity of thought and purpose."

I rose to my feet. A swift new joy surged through me—something exquisite I had not known before. I said:

"Of course, I understand...."

The great heavy door of the vicarage fell shut behind me as I went out into the night. A sense of peace enveloped me. The wind from the moors had blown the clouds into stormy battlements above me. God was waiting.

(To be concluded)

articles.title: Wondering Years
articles.author: Grace Sayre
articles.text:
WONDERING YEARS
Grace Sayre

Time rests her hand; I feel the soft caress
Of fingers laid upon me, as to bless
The moment and the mood, while I may stand
With head bowed to the brief touch of time's hand.

Time lifts her hand and bids me look around
To find myself released, my days unbound;
All the dread that swept my wondering years
Is left to time's vast urn of unshed tears.

articles.title: Button Magic
articles.author: Marijane Morris
articles.text:
BUTTON MAGIC
Marijane Morris

The buttons that my mother left are mine.
They hold enchanted, carefree hours of play.
I know the secrets of each brave design—
They are my mother's life in resume.

These pearls once gleamed upon her wedding dress,
She'd say, a smile remaining on her face.
These baby buttons speak of blessedness
And love that found its home the perfect place.

Two from my father's shirt, ungainly pair;
This odd one was a hero in my eyes;
These wide ones stretched on winter underwear,
All played my childish games in some disguise.

Swift time can never quite escape from me,
Because my mother's buttons hold the key.

articles.title: Food Preservation
articles.author: Bernice Stookey Linford
articles.text:
Food Preservation
Bernice Stookey Linford

Thou shalt be diligent in preserving what thou hast, that thou mayest be a wise steward; for it is the free gift of the Lord thy God, and thou art his steward (D. & C. 136:27).

CANNING is still the most widely used form of food preservation both in the home kitchen and in commercial factories. Yet each year many persons die from food poisoning as a result of poor canning methods due to carelessness or misunderstanding.

Because the American people depend so much on canned products for their daily food, the United States Department of Agriculture has prepared for distribution a great variety of canning and other food preservation bulletins. The utmost care has been taken by specialists in testing and assembling this authentic material. Complete step-by-step directions and illustrations are included, which make canning safe and simple even for the most inexperienced.

Single copies of these bulletins are free and may be obtained by contacting county extension agents or by writing the Office of Information, United States Department of Agriculture. Available publications are listed at the end of this article. Your family needs will determine the specific bulletins you send for—all are authentic and practical.

Freezer lockers and home freezers are available to many. Frozen foods, if the best quality food is selected and proper methods used, are almost the equal of fresh foods. They may be of even better quality than fresh foods which have been shipped great distances.

merged.notes: Split merged file containing a serialized story chapter, two poems, and the beginning of a food preservation article. The Food Preservation article appears to be cut off mid-sentence in the extracted text.

short.title: A Mother's Time Is The Greatest Gift A Man Can Leave His Children
short.author: Beneficial Life Insurance Company
short.text:
A MOTHER'S TIME IS THE GREATEST GIFT A MAN CAN LEAVE HIS CHILDREN

If you should die today, could your wife afford to stay home? Guarantee your children their mother's time by carrying adequate life insurance.

BENEFICIAL LIFE INSURANCE COMPANY

Home Office—Beneficial Life Building
Salt Lake City 1, Utah
George Albert Smith, President
short.notes: This is a complete advertisement for Beneficial Life Insurance Company, not a regular article. The ad features a family photo and promotes life insurance to ensure mothers can stay home with children.

short.title: SYMBOL OF THRIFT
short.text:
SYMBOL OF THRIFT

AMONG priceless heritages left by our pioneer mothers is the custom of providing a bounteous supply of fruits for the long winter months. In this generation, the housewife who stocks her shelves with jars of choice fresh fruits, jams, and jellies, preserved and sweetened with pure, home-produced sugar, has learned well the lesson of prudent care for her family.

Perfect for canning, cooking, freezing, or table use.

UTAH-IDAHO SUGAR COMPANY
short.notes: This is a complete advertisement for Utah-Idaho Sugar Company featuring their U&I Sugar product, with imagery of a mason jar and text about home preservation traditions
